title: Human Reproduction
subtitle: From gamete formation to birth — a visual, NCERT-aligned and NEET-ready guide to the male system, female system, gametogenesis, menstrual cycle, fertilisation, pregnancy, parturition and lactation.

heroModel.title: Human reproduction overview
heroModel.subtitle: Drag to rotate · scroll to zoom

roadmapTitle: The chapter moves from organs to gametes to embryo.
roadmapDescription: Keep this order in mind: reproductive organs make gametes, hormones coordinate cycles, gametes fuse, embryo implants, pregnancy develops, birth and lactation complete the story.
flowchart:
flowchart LR
  A[Male reproductive system] --> B[Spermatogenesis]
  C[Female reproductive system] --> D[Oogenesis]
  D --> E[Menstrual cycle]
  B --> F[Fertilisation]
  E --> F
  F --> G[Cleavage + blastocyst]
  G --> H[Implantation]
  H --> I[Pregnancy + placenta]
  I --> J[Parturition]
  J --> K[Lactation]

roadmap.title: Male reproductive system
roadmap.summary: Testes, ducts, glands and penis form the male reproductive pathway.

roadmap.title: Female reproductive system
roadmap.summary: Ovaries, oviducts, uterus, cervix, vagina and external genitalia support egg formation, fertilisation and pregnancy.

roadmap.title: Spermatogenesis
roadmap.summary: Spermatogonia produce spermatozoa through mitosis, meiosis and spermiogenesis.

roadmap.title: Oogenesis
roadmap.summary: Primary oocytes begin before birth and complete stages only around ovulation and fertilisation.

roadmap.title: Menstrual cycle
roadmap.summary: Hormones coordinate menstrual, follicular, ovulatory and luteal phases.

roadmap.title: Fertilisation and implantation
roadmap.summary: Sperm meets ovum in the ampullary-isthmic junction; blastocyst implants in the endometrium.

roadmap.title: Pregnancy and placenta
roadmap.summary: Placenta supports exchange, endocrine signalling and fetal development.

roadmap.title: Parturition and lactation
roadmap.summary: Foetal ejection reflex and oxytocin drive birth; prolactin supports milk production.

conceptDescription: Every major heading is converted into a study card with the NEET line students usually miss.

sections.title: Testis, ducts, glands and penis
sections.description: The male reproductive system produces sperm, stores and transports them, and adds glandular secretions to form semen.
points: Testes lie in the scrotum because sperm formation needs a temperature lower than core body temperature.
Seminiferous tubules contain germ cells and Sertoli cells.
Leydig cells in interstitial spaces secrete androgens, mainly testosterone.
Epididymis, vas deferens, ejaculatory duct and urethra form the sperm transport pathway.
sections.neet: Sertoli cells nourish germ cells; Leydig cells produce testosterone.

sections.title: Ovary, oviduct, uterus and vagina
sections.description: The female system produces ova and provides sites for fertilisation, implantation, pregnancy and birth.
points: Ovaries contain follicles at different stages and secrete estrogen and progesterone.
Oviduct has infundibulum, ampulla and isthmus.
Fertilisation usually occurs at the ampullary-isthmic junction.
Uterus has perimetrium, myometrium and endometrium.
sections.neet: Endometrium undergoes cyclic changes and is the implantation site for the blastocyst.

sections.title: Spermatogenesis and sperm structure
sections.description: Sperm production starts at puberty and converts diploid spermatogonia into haploid spermatozoa.
points: Spermatogonia divide by mitosis to maintain stem cells and produce primary spermatocytes.
Meiosis I forms secondary spermatocytes; meiosis II forms spermatids.
Spermiogenesis converts spermatids into spermatozoa.
A sperm has head, neck, middle piece and tail; acrosome contains enzymes for ovum penetration.
sections.neet: Spermiation is release of sperm from Sertoli cells into the lumen, not the same as spermiogenesis.

sections.title: Oogenesis and follicular development
sections.description: Oogenesis begins before birth, pauses, resumes at puberty and completes only after sperm entry.
points: Oogonia form primary oocytes during fetal life.
Primary oocytes are arrested in prophase I until puberty.
A secondary oocyte is released during ovulation and is arrested in metaphase II.
Completion of meiosis II occurs only if fertilisation happens.
sections.neet: The ovum released at ovulation is technically a secondary oocyte.

sections.title: Menstrual cycle
sections.description: The menstrual cycle is a hormone-linked cycle of ovarian and uterine changes.
points: Menstrual phase: endometrium breaks down if fertilisation does not occur.
Follicular phase: follicles grow and estrogen rises.
LH surge triggers ovulation around the middle of the cycle.
Luteal phase: corpus luteum secretes progesterone to maintain endometrium.
sections.neet: LH surge is the immediate trigger for ovulation.

sections.title: Fertilisation and cleavage
sections.description: Fertilisation restores diploidy and starts embryonic development through cleavage divisions.
points: Capacitated sperm reaches the secondary oocyte.
Acrosomal enzymes help sperm penetrate ovum coverings.
Fusion of gamete membranes forms zygote.
Cleavage produces morula and then blastocyst.
sections.neet: Fertilisation normally occurs at the ampullary-isthmic junction of the oviduct.

sections.title: Implantation, placenta and embryonic development
sections.description: Blastocyst attaches to endometrium and placenta becomes the exchange and endocrine interface.
points: Blastocyst embeds in the endometrium during implantation.
Placenta exchanges gases, nutrients and wastes between mother and fetus.
Placenta secretes hCG, hPL, estrogen, progesterone and relaxin.
Umbilical cord connects fetus to placenta.
sections.neet: hCG maintains corpus luteum in early pregnancy and is detected in pregnancy tests.

sections.title: Parturition and lactation
sections.description: Birth is controlled by neuroendocrine feedback; milk production starts after delivery.
points: Foetal ejection reflex begins from the fully developed fetus and placenta.
Oxytocin causes strong uterine contractions.
Positive feedback strengthens contractions until delivery.
Colostrum is rich in antibodies and protects the newborn.
sections.neet: Parturition is a neuroendocrine mechanism driven strongly by oxytocin.

modelDescription: Original Three.js schematics help you understand shape, pathway and sequence without copied textbook diagrams.

models.description: Testis, duct, gland and transport logic.

models.title: Female reproductive pathway
models.description: Ovary, oviduct, uterus and implantation zone.

models.title: Spermatogenesis lab
models.description: Seminiferous tubule and sperm release concept.

models.title: Menstrual cycle wheel
models.description: Menstrual, follicular, ovulatory and luteal phases.

highYield: Testes are outside the abdominal cavity to maintain lower temperature for spermatogenesis.
Seminiferous tubules contain male germ cells and Sertoli cells.
Spermatogenesis starts at puberty; oogenesis starts before birth.
Spermiogenesis is conversion of spermatids into spermatozoa.
Spermiation is release of spermatozoa from Sertoli cells.
Acrosome contains enzymes that help sperm penetrate ovum coverings.
Fertilisation occurs at the ampullary-isthmic junction.
Placenta secretes hCG, hPL, estrogen, progesterone and relaxin.
hCG is detected in pregnancy tests.
Oxytocin causes uterine contractions during parturition.

mistakes.title: Spermiogenesis vs spermiation
mistakes.body: Spermiogenesis changes spermatids into spermatozoa; spermiation releases them from Sertoli cells.

mistakes.title: Ovum at ovulation
mistakes.body: The released cell is a secondary oocyte arrested in metaphase II, not a fully completed ovum.

mistakes.title: Fertilisation site
mistakes.body: The standard NCERT answer is ampullary-isthmic junction, not simply uterus or ovary.

quiz.question: Which cells secrete testosterone in the testis?
quiz.explain: Leydig cells, present in interstitial spaces, produce testosterone.

quiz.question: The immediate trigger for ovulation is:
quiz.explain: A surge in LH around mid-cycle triggers ovulation.

quiz.question: Fertilisation normally occurs in the:
quiz.explain: NCERT specifies the ampullary-isthmic junction of the oviduct.

quiz.question: Spermiogenesis refers to:
quiz.explain: Spermiogenesis is the transformation of spermatids into spermatozoa.

quiz.question: Which hormone is detected in pregnancy tests?
quiz.explain: hCG appears in maternal blood/urine after implantation.

quiz.question: The acrosome is important because it:
quiz.explain: Acrosomal enzymes help sperm penetrate the ovum coverings.

quiz.question: Corpus luteum mainly secretes:
quiz.explain: Progesterone maintains the endometrium in the luteal phase.

quiz.question: Implantation is embedding of:
quiz.explain: The blastocyst embeds in the uterine endometrium.

quiz.question: Parturition is mainly driven by:
quiz.explain: Oxytocin strengthens uterine contractions in a positive feedback loop.

quiz.question: Colostrum is valuable because it is rich in:
quiz.explain: Colostrum provides passive immunity to the newborn.

flashcards.back: Nourishes developing male germ cells inside seminiferous tubules.
Interstitial testis cell that secretes testosterone.
Mid-cycle hormonal spike that triggers ovulation.
Placental hormone used in pregnancy detection.
Attachment and embedding of blastocyst in endometrium.
Birth process caused by neuroendocrine reflex and oxytocin-driven contractions.

revisionTable: Spermatogonia → primary spermatocyte → secondary spermatocyte → spermatid → spermatozoa
Oogonia → primary oocyte → secondary oocyte → ovum after fertilisation
Sperm + secondary oocyte at ampullary-isthmic junction
Foetal ejection reflex → oxytocin → uterine contractions
